description: Hand-curated Widespread Panic song origins written in Burnthday's own voice — facts extracted from fan newsletters (Moon Times / The Panicle), band interviews, and the web, then paraphrased into original prose with sources cited. This is facts-into-original-prose, NOT republication. It is a supplement to the Facebook-sourced song-origins.json: entries with mode 'new' are songs not yet in that set; mode 'augment' entries add or correct facts on an existing origin without duplicating what is already there. The build merges these by slug.

conventions.mode: 'new' = a song not in song-origins.json; 'augment' = adds/corrects facts on an existing entry
conventions.sources: Every factual claim is traceable to a listed source. Unverifiable claims are omitted (evidence discipline).
conventions.attribution: Moon Times = official WSP newsletter (Internet Archive); The Panicle = fan transcription (widespread-panic.blogspot.com); interviews credited to outlet + date.

origins.title: North
origins.text:
“The song was written by Jerry Joseph a long time ago, probably when we first met him, around 16 years ago.”
—John Bell, jambands.com, 4/30/2010

“A lot of that I wrote in Nicaragua... I still had this kinda idealic vision of the Northwest. [laughing] Burns out really quickly. As soon as you're there for three days in the rain you're like, ‘what the fuck was I thinking.’”
—Jerry Joseph, “Passion and Pain” interview

# of times played: 287 First time played: 9/25/99
Notes: A Jerry Joseph cover; one of two covers on Dirty Side Down (2010). Warren Haynes sat in on it at Wanee, 2010.

sources.label: Jerry Joseph, “Passion and Pain” interview (Part I)
John Bell, "True to Our Nature" interview by Randy Ray

origins.title: Time Zones
origins.text:
“It's funny because when we were writing them, Jerry and I thought ‘Time Zones’ would be the big new Panic anthem and ‘Second Skin’ would be this Massive Attack-style jam, but once they were played live, they kind of flipped roles.”
—Dave Schools, American Songwriter, 2006

“With ‘Time Zones,’ which I thought was real hip, there were references to To Kill a Mockingbird. So I went to the library and got the Cliff Notes and the book...”
—John Bell, American Songwriter, 2006

# of times played: 126 First time played: 4/30/05
Notes: Written by Schools & Jerry Joseph in the same session as “Second Skin.” On Earth to America (2005).

sources.label: Dave Schools & John Bell, “New Frontiers” by Andy Tennille

origins.title: Her Dance Needs No Body
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (music seeded by Jimmy Herring)
origins.text:
“This song is more deserving of just verse chorus, verse chorus, bridge and solo and out kind of thing... the musical beginnings of ‘Three Candles’ and ‘Her Dance’... coming from Jimmy [Herring].”
—John Bell, “Tickle the Truth” (Glide), 2008

# of times played: 40 First time played: 4/1/08

origins.title: Raise The Roof
origins.text:
“The song came from Mikey's direction and we all just hauntingly followed along during the demo sessions, and cleaned up the vocals on the demos, and left it like that... There's no place really to go except maybe put some more guitars on, add a third vocal part... In essence, it had what it had, you know?”
—John Bell, Rock & Road interview, Atlanta, 12/15/94

# of times played: 45 First time played: 1/27/94
Notes: On Ain't Life Grand (1994); mostly retired after 2001 (last on 6/22/01).

sources.label: John Bell, R&R interview, Grady's Restaurant, Atlanta

origins.title: Three Candles
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (music seeded by Jimmy Herring)
origins.text:
“The musical beginnings of ‘Three Candles’ and ‘Her Dance’... coming from Jimmy [Herring]. He had a bunch of great ideas that had already been cooking.”
—John Bell, “Tickle the Truth” (Glide), 2008

# of times played: 33 First time played: 4/1/08

origins.title: From The Cradle
origins.text:
# of times played: 125 First time played: 4/22/06
Notes: A Todd Nance song. Per American Songwriter (2006), its imagery evokes the aftermath of Hurricane Katrina, though Nance wrote it before the storm. On Earth to America (2005).

origins.title: Gomero Blanco
origins.text:
“Gomero Blanco: First recorded during the May 1987 sessions at John Keene's Studio in Athens for the initial release of ‘Space Wrangler.’ While this song is not played live, several shows at the Uptown Lounge featured similar sounding jams.”
—The Panicle, Vol. 1 #5, July 1992

Notes: A studio-era track; not a live staple.

origins.title: Driving Song
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (band-credited; biographically a John Bell / Michael Houser co-write)
origins.text:
# of times played: 831 First time played: 4/7/86
Notes: A Widespread Panic original; biographically a John Bell / Michael Houser co-write from their UGA-roommate days. On Space Wrangler (1988). Source: Everyday Companion; The Ticket Blog.

origins.title: Rock
origins.text:
# of times played: 734 First time played: 10/4/89
Notes: A Widespread Panic original, also titled “Brass Shoes, Walking Blues.” On the 1991 self-titled album (“Mom's Kitchen”). Source: Everyday Companion.

origins.title: Tall Boy
origins.text:
# of times played: 512 First time played: 8/3/95
Notes: A Widespread Panic original, also titled “Naked In The Mud.” On Bombs & Butterflies (1997). Source: Everyday Companion.

sources.label: Everyday Companion — Song Histories & Originals index

origins.title: Better Off
origins.text:
# of times played: 340 First time played: 2/7/92
Notes: A Widespread Panic original co-credited to T Lavitz, the Dixie Dregs keyboardist who played with the band in 1991. On Everyday (1993). Source: Everyday Companion; Wikipedia.

origins.title: Bear's Gone Fishin'
origins.text:
# of times played: 248 First time played: 7/1/97
Notes: Debuted as an instrumental in July 1997; lyrics added ~two years later. Named for the band's Colorado friend Thomas “Bear” Guenther (d. 2019) — the same “Bear” behind “Postcard.” On 'Til the Medicine Takes (1999). Source: JamBase; Dave Schools, Colorado Music Hall of Fame induction speech.

sources.label: Widespread Panic at Red Rocks feature (Bear Guenther, instrumental-to-lyrics timeline)
Dave Schools, Colorado Music Hall of Fame induction speech

origins.title: Surprise Valley
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (a signature Michael Houser piece)
origins.text:
# of times played: 390 First time played: 6/27/99
Notes: A Widespread Panic original, one of Michael Houser's signature pieces, tied to the band's Colorado connection. On 'Til the Medicine Takes (1999). Source: Lockn' Festival.

sources.label: “7 Widespread Panic Stories Told By the Band” (Houser signature song; Colorado connection)

origins.title: Big Wooly Mammoth
origins.text:
# of times played: 275 First time played: 10/10/95
Notes: A Widespread Panic original sung by JoJo Hermann; a long-time live favorite finally cut for Don't Tell the Band (2001). Source: Wikipedia.

sources.label: Don't Tell the Band (JoJo Hermann vocal; long-time crowd favorite)

origins.title: Blue Indian
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (counted among Michael Houser's songs)
origins.text:
# of times played: 311 First time played: 10/6/98
Notes: A Widespread Panic original (counted among Michael Houser's songs). The album title 'Til the Medicine Takes is drawn from a line in its chorus. Source: Wikipedia; Lockn' Festival.

sources.label: 'Til the Medicine Takes (album title drawn from the chorus)

origins.title: Send Your Mind
origins.text:
# of times played: 342 First time played: 4/2/91
Notes: A Van Morrison cover (from his late-'60s Bang Records sides). On the 1991 self-titled album (“Mom's Kitchen”). Source: Everyday Companion.

origins.title: Sleeping Man
origins.text:
# of times played: 246 First time played: 3/21/95
Notes: A Vic Chesnutt cover (from his 1993 album Drunk). Source: Everyday Companion.

origins.title: Aunt Avis
origins.text:
“Vic Chestnutt deserves to win Grammy after Grammy. He's the Bob Dylan of the ’90s. We just made a video with Billy Bob. He directed the video for the song ‘Aunt Avis.’ Laura Dern is in it. Vic is in it. It was filmed in Arkansas.”
—Dave Schools, Centerstage, 5/4/01

# of times played: 268 First time played: 9/9/95
Notes: A Vic Chesnutt song; on Bombs & Butterflies (1997). Thornton also directed a 1991 WSP documentary.

sources.label: Debut date & Billy Bob Thornton-directed video
Billy Bob Thornton video details (Laura Dern; Arkansas; 1991 WSP documentary)
sources.publisher: Centerstage (2001) & South Bend Tribune (1997), via Spreadnet

origins.title: Bowlegged Woman
origins.text:
# of times played: 242 First time played: 2/2/87
Notes: A blues cover written by Calvin Carter and Bobby Rush (full title “Bowlegged Woman, Knock-Kneed Man”); also cut by Hot Tuna. Source: Everyday Companion.

origins.title: Henry Parsons Died
origins.text:
“It's a song about a very, very bad man. What more can you say?... I just like to rock the f*** out of that song, I love it! It just kicks!”
—Dave Schools, “Rock & Road” interview, 4/1/95

# of times played: 709 First time played: 1/27/89
Notes: A Bloodkin song (Daniel Hutchens / Eric Carter); appears on Everyday (1993).

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Chainsaw City
origins.text:
# of times played: 167 First time played: 6/23/95
Notes: A Jerry Joseph song, originally on his band Little Women's “Pretty Wiped Out” (1990). Source: Everyday Companion.

origins.title: Quarter Tank Of Gasoline
origins.text:
# of times played: 33 First time played: 7/5/99
Notes: A Bloodkin song (Daniel Hutchens), from “Good Luck Charm” (1994). Source: Everyday Companion.

origins.title: Party At Your Mama's House
origins.text:
# of times played: 227 First time played: 5/7/97
Notes: A Widespread Panic instrumental, also known as “That Thang.” On 'Til the Medicine Takes (1999). Source: Everyday Companion.

sources.label: Everyday Companion — Song Histories (instrumental; a.k.a. “That Thang”)

origins.title: Little Lilly
origins.text:
# of times played: 242 First time played: 11/17/98
Notes: A Widespread Panic original; the opener on Don't Tell the Band (2001), full-titled “Little Lilly (A.K.A. First Snow Or Blue Girl).” Source: Jambands.com; lyric databases.

sources.label: Don't Tell the Band review (Beatles-lyric wink)
Full title / alternate names (“First Snow,” “Blue Girl”)
sources.publisher: lyric databases; corroborated by fan alias sheets

origins.title: Down
origins.text:
# of times played: 141 First time played: 4/16/00
Notes: A Widespread Panic original on Don't Tell the Band (2001); the song that marked Todd Nance's emergence as a lead singer. Source: Jambands.com.

sources.label: Don't Tell the Band review (Nance's lead-vocal emergence)

origins.title: Give
origins.text:
# of times played: 235 First time played: 4/7/00
Notes: A Widespread Panic original on Don't Tell the Band (2001), built on one of Michael Houser's most aggressive guitar figures. Source: Jambands.com.

origins.title: Casa Del Grillo
origins.text:
# of times played: 89 First time played: 7/16/00
Notes: A Widespread Panic original on Don't Tell the Band (2001) with Spanish lyrics, extending Domingo Ortiz's Latin influence; John Keane added guitar effects. Source: Jambands.com; Wikipedia.

sources.label: Don't Tell the Band review (Spanish lyrics; Ortiz's Latin influence)

origins.title: Sleepy Monkey
origins.text:
“We drove to Atlanta from our King Avenue house to pick up a box of 500 45's from the presser.”
—John Bell, via JamBase

# of times played: 245 First time played: 2/24/85
Notes: The band's self-released 1986 debut 45 on their own Space Baby label (500 copies); a shortened “Monkey Image” was the B-side.

sources.label: Widespread Panic debut-single reissue (Space Baby label, 500 copies, JB's account)

origins.title: Wondering
origins.text:
“‘Wondering’ is a song Mikey wrote. We never really knew what to do with it until the ‘Every Day’ sessions. That song is one of the [few] examples of a song that we just hammered and hammered and hammered in the studio. I think by the end the take that was used on the record was like the forty-eighth take... It's just one of those ‘Hey man, what are you up to?’ kind of songs!”
—Dave Schools, “Rock & Road” interview, 4/1/95

# of times played: 666 First time played: 2/7/92
Notes: Appears on Everyday (1993).

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Pilgrims
origins.text:
“It's a song about riding from town to town. You're listening to a song on the radio and it fades out, and then another city's radio station fades in over top of it. It's like the same song ’cause we're travelling all the time, and the radio becomes a song in and of itself.”
—Dave Schools, “Rock & Road” interview, 4/1/95

# of times played: 575 First time played: 9/14/92

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Makes Sense To Me
origins.text:
“That's one that Danny Hutchins wrote, for Bloodkin, and actually after they played it he thought to himself that it sounded more like us. So he presented it to us, and we liked it, ’cause we like those guys a whole lot. And it works for us.”
—Dave Schools, “Rock & Road” interview, 4/1/95

# of times played: 673 First time played: 4/1/91
Notes: A Bloodkin (Daniel Hutchens) song. Source: Everyday Companion.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Can't Get High
origins.text:
“Another great Danny song. [It's] about whatever you need to satisfy your soul, not being there. Not being able to feel feelings of elation or euphoria because whatever you need to do that is not there. I.E. a girlfriend, your pet, some sleep, whatever.”
—Dave Schools, “Rock & Road” interview, 4/1/95

# of times played: 372 First time played: 7/9/93
Notes: A Bloodkin (Daniel Hutchens) song; the band's radio single, performed on Late Night with Conan O'Brien (1995).

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Junior
origins.text:
“‘Junior’ is a Junior Kimbrough song. It's actually a medley of two or three Junior Kimbrough songs... plays blues, and uh, it's a great song. It's a great riff... It's just a rocket blues song.”
—Dave Schools, “Rock & Road” interview, 4/1/95

# of times played: 454 First time played: 4/9/93
Notes: A cover of the north Mississippi hill-country bluesman Junior Kimbrough.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Love Tractor
origins.text:
“‘Love Tractor,’ musically it's a lick that we used to jam around on... because it sounded so much like the Athens band Love Tractor. Who is a great band and we're all big fans... we were calling it ‘That Love Tractor Thing.’ [So] JB wrote a song about a guy who loves his tractor. Just loves riding around on his tractor!”
—Dave Schools, “Rock & Road” interview, 4/1/95

# of times played: 993 First time played: 6/16/89

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Dream Song
origins.text:
“‘Dream Song’ was a lullabye that Mike wrote... to his child, Waker, when he was a baby. I'm not even sure if he wrote the words or if JB took an idea, lyrically, that Mike had, and actually wrote the words. It's a beautiful song. It's one I get to play fretless on.”
—Dave Schools, “Rock & Road” interview, 4/1/95

# of times played: 204 First time played: 3/13/92

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Little Kin
origins.text:
“‘Little Kin’ is a... lyrically I can't really go into it too much; it's one of JB's and it seems highly personal... I luuuuv that song!... it's kind of personal to me too because I'm an adopted child... I guess it's about childhood and learning to deal with the things of life. As far as a bass player, I love it cause it's just a slab of greasy, raw funk.”
—Dave Schools, “Rock & Road” interview, 4/1/95

# of times played: 444 First time played: 4/24/93

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Vacation
origins.text:
“There was one we wanted to put on the record called ‘Vacation’ that we had never recorded before but have been playing live on and off for eight years. We did one take of it, and John Keane comes back on the monitor and says, ‘Well that was nine and a half minutes long.’ So we knew that wasn't really a possibility.”
—Dave Schools, The Music Monitor #94, May 1997

# of times played: 142 First time played: 9/15/86
Notes: Nearly recorded for Bombs & Butterflies (1997).

sources.label: Dave Schools, The Music Monitor #94 (Pat Lall), May 1997, via Spreadnet

origins.title: Cotton Was King
origins.text:
“It's a tribute to New Orleans — past, present, and future. Yeah, that was fun to write... it uses the phrase ‘before the beginning,’ which I actually got from Firesign Theatre... It dates back to the beginning of time that a place like New Orleans was always meant to be created. It was inevitable — a geographic hot spot where things were going to happen no matter what, and New Orleans is what manifested.”
—John Bell, jambands.com, 4/30/2010

# of times played: 121 First time played: 5/5/10
Notes: Closing track on Dirty Side Down (2010).

sources.label: John Bell, "True to Our Nature" interview by Randy Ray

origins.title: St. Louis
origins.text:
“We've been playing that on stage as a little bit of a transition piece. It took on a little more solid shape in the studio. There's a free-flowing, open, airy jam there in the middle of it... Oh, let's just stick the ‘St. Louis’ jam in front of that [‘Shut Up and Drive’], and you've got a long intro then.”
—John Bell, jambands.com, 4/30/2010

# of times played: 71 First time played: 10/19/07
Notes: Instrumental; on Dirty Side Down (2010).

sources.label: John Bell, "True to Our Nature" interview by Randy Ray

origins.title: Shut Up And Drive
origins.text:
Asked whether “Shut Up and Drive” fulfills the title's potential as a great road tune: “That's what it was meant for.”
—John Bell, jambands.com, 4/30/2010

# of times played: 110 First time played: 5/8/10
Notes: On Dirty Side Down (2010); paired with the instrumental “St. Louis” as its intro.

sources.label: John Bell, "True to Our Nature" interview by Randy Ray

origins.title: Postcard
origins.text:
“When the band started we all lived in this little house in Athens, Georgia, called King Avenue, and we had a friend. His name was Tom Gunther. We commonly knew him as Bear. He left town... Some months later we got a postcard. It arrived at the band house. On the front side of it was a skier catching air way up in the mountains. On the back side was a post mark from Telluride, Colorado, and a very simple message on the postcard that said, ‘The air here is great. We're having an ass-kicking time. This town is nuts. My kind of place. I don't ever want to leave.’”
—Dave Schools, Colorado Music Hall of Fame induction speech

“...we had a buddy that wrote us a postcard and that turned into a song about 7 years ago. He gets little 15 cent royalty checks.”
—John Bell, KGSR (Austin), 4/12/97

# of times played: 257 First time played: 10/6/86
Notes: “Bear” is Tom Guenther — the same friend behind “Bear's Gone Fishin'.”

sources.label: Dave Schools, Colorado Music Hall of Fame induction speech
John Bell & Todd Nance, KGSR interview, 4/12/97 (the royalty-check aside)
